methodology: Combined quantitative factor analysis with qualitative event signal assessment. Tier 1 and Tier 2 countries covered with 11 key factors each. High-impact countries (USA, CHN, JPN, IRN, ISR, PAK, ARE, QAT, KWT) received detailed multi-paragraph analysis; stable countries received efficient assessments. All estimates incorporate the week's transformative events: US-Israeli strikes killing Iran's Khamenei, IRGC retaliation across Gulf, Strait of Hormuz closure, Pakistan-Afghanistan war, SCOTUS IEEPA tariff ruling, China-Japan sanctions escalation, and global market turmoil.
key_events_this_week: US-Israel strikes kill Iran Supreme Leader Khamenei (Feb 28) - transformative
IRGC retaliates against 27 US bases across Gulf region (Mar 1) - transformative
Strait of Hormuz effectively closed to commercial shipping (Mar 1) - transformative
Pakistan declares open war on Afghanistan (Feb 27) - transformative
US Supreme Court strikes down IEEPA tariffs (Feb 20) - transformative
China sanctions 50 Japanese companies, rare earth controls (Feb 25) - major
Global stock markets tumble; gold surges past $5,300/oz (Mar 1) - major
Argentina Senate approves landmark labor reform (Feb 27) - major
South Korea sentences ex-President Yoon to life (Feb 19) - major
US-Indonesia sign $33 billion trade agreement (Feb 19) - major
Hezbollah breaks ceasefire, fires on northern Israel (Mar 1) - major
Thailand PM Anutin forms coalition government (Feb 13) - moderate

estimates.reasoning: US GDP growth at 2% with 2026 forecast of 2.1%. SCOTUS IEEPA tariff ruling reduces trade policy drag but Section 122 tariff creates uncertainty. Iran crisis and oil price surge pose downside risk to consumer spending. Fed rate cuts support growth.
supporting_evidence: SCOTUS ruling lowered effective tariff rate from 16% to 9.1%
Oil price surge from Hormuz closure adds input cost pressure
counter_arguments: Section 122 tariff limited to 150 days creates policy cliff
Hormuz oil shock could reduce growth 0.3-0.5pp if sustained
estimates.investor_implication: US growth resilient near 2% but downside risks from oil shock and tariff uncertainty warrant caution on cyclical sectors.

estimates.reasoning: Inflation at 2.7% faces upward pressure from oil price surge (Brent $80-90+) following Hormuz closure. IMF had forecast 2.4% for 2026 but this was pre-crisis. Energy pass-through could add 0.3-0.5pp.
supporting_evidence: Brent crude surged from ~$73 to $80-90 range
Gold at record $5,300+ signals inflation fears
counter_arguments: SCOTUS tariff ruling reduces goods inflation from lower tariffs
Fed remains hawkish with rate at 4.5%
estimates.investor_implication: Inflation uptick from energy prices may delay Fed rate cuts; TIPS and energy equities offer inflation hedges.

estimates.reasoning: US current account deficit at -4.12% of GDP. Lower tariffs from SCOTUS ruling could widen deficit via increased imports, but higher oil prices increase energy import bill. Net effect roughly neutral.
supporting_evidence: SCOTUS ruling reduces tariff barriers, potentially increasing imports
Higher oil prices increase energy import costs
estimates.investor_implication: Current account deficit likely stable; trade-sensitive sectors face mixed signals from tariff restructuring.

estimates.reasoning: US debt at 125% of GDP continues upward trajectory. Military operations against Iran add defense spending. SCOTUS tariff ruling reduces customs revenue by approximately $175B annually. Fiscal consolidation remains distant.
supporting_evidence: Iran military operations increase defense expenditure
SCOTUS ruling eliminates ~$175B in tariff revenue
One Big Beautiful Bill Act adds fiscal commitments
counter_arguments: Higher oil prices increase energy tax revenues
estimates.investor_implication: Rising debt trajectory confirmed; Treasury supply concerns may pressure long-term yields. Watch 10Y auction dynamics.

estimates.reasoning: US political stability already low at 0.03 faces further pressure from military conflict with Iran, partisan divide over strikes, and ongoing tariff policy upheaval from SCOTUS ruling. International criticism at UNSC adds diplomatic friction.
supporting_evidence: Military conflict with Iran divides domestic opinion
SCOTUS vs Executive branch tension over trade authority
UN condemnation of strikes from multiple allies
counter_arguments: Institutional checks functioning (SCOTUS ruling demonstrates rule of law)
Bipartisan support for Iran operations from key congressional leaders
estimates.investor_implication: Elevated political risk premium on US assets; institutional strength provides floor but policy unpredictability persists.

estimates.reasoning: US military spending at 3.4% of GDP will increase with active operations against Iran, forward deployment in Middle East, and potential escalation costs. Defense supplemental likely.
supporting_evidence: Active military operations in Iran (Operation Epic Fury)
Forward deployment to protect Gulf shipping lanes
Increased missile defense costs from IRGC retaliation
counter_arguments: DOGE efficiency efforts could offset some costs
estimates.investor_implication: Defense sector stocks benefit; Lockheed Martin, Raytheon, Northrop Grumman see sustained demand signal.

estimates.reasoning: US trade openness at 30% of GDP may increase as SCOTUS IEEPA ruling effectively lowered average tariff rate from 16% to 9.1%. Section 122 tariff is temporary (150 days). Long-term trade liberalization direction depends on congressional action.
supporting_evidence: SCOTUS struck down IEEPA tariffs, reducing trade barriers
Hundreds of companies suing for $134B+ in tariff refunds
US-Indonesia $33B trade deal opens new market access
counter_arguments: Trump imposed Section 122 10-15% tariff as stopgap
China tariffs remain elevated with additional 10% increase
estimates.investor_implication: Trade openness improving structurally from SCOTUS ruling; importers and retailers benefit from lower input costs.

estimates.reasoning: FDI inflows to US remain resilient given safe-haven status and strong dollar, but geopolitical uncertainty from Iran conflict and tariff policy upheaval may cause some investment deferrals.
supporting_evidence: Strong dollar makes US assets attractive
SCOTUS tariff ruling reduces trade policy uncertainty
US remains world's top FDI destination
counter_arguments: Iran conflict creates geopolitical risk premium
Policy uncertainty from tariff regime change
estimates.investor_implication: FDI flows resilient; manufacturing investment may pause pending tariff clarity but tech and services remain strong.

estimates.reasoning: USD strengthened as safe-haven during Iran crisis. DXY rally from flight to safety. Fed rate differential supports dollar.
supporting_evidence: Dollar surged as safe haven during Iran crisis
Fed rate at 4.5% maintains interest rate differential
Treasury demand increases in risk-off environment
counter_arguments: Potential Fed rate cuts later in 2026
Current account deficit fundamentally bearish for USD
estimates.investor_implication: Strong dollar benefits importers and consumers; pressures EM currencies and US exporters.

estimates.reasoning: US investment risk score (higher = more risk) increases due to active military conflict, oil shock, tariff policy uncertainty, and rising debt. Partially offset by strong institutions and SCOTUS demonstration of rule of law.
supporting_evidence: Active military conflict in Middle East
Oil supply shock from Hormuz closure
Tariff policy in transition after SCOTUS ruling
estimates.investor_implication: Risk-adjusted returns in US decline near-term; defensive positioning and inflation hedges warranted.

estimates.reasoning: US relations with key partners strained by Iran strikes. UN allies (France, Spain) condemned actions. China-US tensions elevated with tariff escalation. Canada faces tariff implementation.
supporting_evidence: Spain PM condemned strikes as breach of international law
France, Russia, China called UNSC emergency session
Additional 10% tariff on China effective March 4
counter_arguments: US-Indonesia $33B trade deal shows bilateral engagement
estimates.investor_implication: Diplomatic capital expenditure high; relationship friction may limit multilateral cooperation and trade negotiations.

estimates.reasoning: China GDP growth at 4.8% forecast to slow to 4.2% in 2026. US added 10% tariff effective March 4. Deflationary pressures persist with CPI at 0%. Property sector remains weak. However, SCOTUS tariff ruling reduces worst-case trade war scenarios.
supporting_evidence: Additional 10% US tariff on Chinese goods from March 4
CPI at 0% reflects weak domestic demand
counter_arguments: SCOTUS ruling weakened US tariff authority, benefiting China's trade position
estimates.investor_implication: China growth decelerating but controllable; stimulus-dependent sectors (infrastructure, SOEs) outperform private sector.

estimates.reasoning: China CPI at 0% (borderline deflation) expected to edge up to 0.7% in 2026 per IMF. Oil price surge from Hormuz crisis adds imported inflation but weak domestic demand limits pass-through.
supporting_evidence: Oil price surge adds cost pressure
counter_arguments: Weak consumer demand limits price pass-through
estimates.investor_implication: Deflationary risk remains primary concern; reflation trades premature until domestic demand recovers.

estimates.reasoning: China current account surplus at 2.26% of GDP. Export competitiveness maintained but US tariff escalation and supply chain diversification pressure trade volumes. Hormuz crisis disrupts energy imports.
supporting_evidence: SCOTUS ruling may reduce future tariff burden
counter_arguments: US tariff escalation reduces China exports to US
Hormuz closure raises energy import costs
estimates.investor_implication: Current account surplus under modest pressure; exporters face margin compression from tariffs and energy costs.

estimates.reasoning: China government debt at 96.3% of GDP continues rising trajectory as fiscal stimulus required to offset property sector weakness and trade headwinds. Local government debt adds to total burden.
supporting_evidence: Local government financing vehicles adding hidden debt
counter_arguments: Large domestic savings pool finances debt
State control of banking system manages rollover risk
estimates.investor_implication: Debt trajectory concerning but manageable given domestic financing; watch LGFV refinancing risks.

estimates.reasoning: China's political stability score at -0.51 remains stable under centralized CCP control. Iran crisis provides diplomatic opportunity as China positions against US unilateralism. Japan sanctions demonstrate assertive foreign policy.
supporting_evidence: CCP centralized control maintains domestic stability
Diplomatic alignment with Russia on Iran condemns US strikes
Sanctions against 50 Japanese companies shows policy assertiveness
counter_arguments: Economic slowdown could generate social discontent
estimates.investor_implication: Political stability maintained through authoritarian control; investor risk is policy unpredictability rather than instability.

estimates.reasoning: China military spending at 1.7% of GDP trending upward. Taiwan tensions, Japan confrontation, and Middle East instability reinforce defense buildup rationale. PLA modernization continues.
counter_arguments: Economic slowdown may constrain spending growth
Official figures likely understate true spending
estimates.investor_implication: Defense spending growth sustained; Chinese defense contractors and dual-use tech firms benefit.

estimates.reasoning: China trade openness at 37.2% (lowest in two decades) continues declining trend. US tariff escalation, Japan sanctions, and supply chain diversification reduce trade integration. Domestic circulation strategy accelerates.
supporting_evidence: Trade openness at two-decade low per pipeline data
Sanctions on 50 Japanese companies reduce bilateral trade
counter_arguments: ASEAN and Belt and Road trade growing
SCOTUS ruling may reduce long-term US tariff burden
Export competitiveness in EVs, solar, batteries expanding
estimates.investor_implication: China decoupling from Western economies accelerating; investors should differentiate China-domestic vs China-export exposure.

estimates.reasoning: FDI into China declining as geopolitical tensions with US and Japan intensify. Sanctions on Japanese companies signal hostile environment for foreign business. Supply chain diversification to Vietnam, India accelerating.
supporting_evidence: Sanctions on 50 Japanese companies deter foreign investment
US-China tariff escalation increases operating costs
Supply chain diversification to ASEAN ongoing
counter_arguments: Some sectors (EVs, green energy) still draw investment
estimates.investor_implication: FDI outflows accelerating; multinational China+1 strategies becoming China-exit for some sectors.

estimates.reasoning: CNY at 6.90/USD faces depreciation pressure from US tariff escalation, capital outflows, and interest rate differential (PBoC at 3.1% vs Fed at 4.5%). PBoC managing orderly depreciation.
supporting_evidence: Trade tensions increase capital outflow pressure
PBoC has historically managed controlled depreciation
counter_arguments: Large FX reserves provide intervention capacity
PBoC daily fixing mechanism controls pace
estimates.investor_implication: Managed CNY depreciation likely; exporters benefit but capital outflow risk increases for foreign investors.

estimates.reasoning: China investment risk increasing from trade tensions (US tariffs, Japan sanctions), deflationary pressures, property sector stress, and geopolitical positioning. Partially offset by policy support and market size.
supporting_evidence: US tariff escalation raises trade risk
Japan sanctions signal broader economic warfare
estimates.investor_implication: China risk premium rising; selective exposure to policy-supported sectors (tech, green energy) preferred over broad market.

estimates.reasoning: China's key bilateral relationships deteriorating. US tariff escalation continues. Japan sanctions mark sharp escalation. UNSC confrontation over Iran. Only Russia relationship strengthening.
supporting_evidence: Sanctions on 50 Japanese companies including rare earth controls
UNSC diplomatic confrontation with Western powers over Iran
counter_arguments: Trump-Xi summit expected April 2026 offers dialogue opportunity
estimates.investor_implication: Relationship deterioration across Western partners; China pivoting to Global South creates investment opportunities in BRI corridor.

estimates.reasoning: Japan GDP growth at 1.1% forecast to slow to 0.6% in 2026. China sanctions on 50 Japanese companies and rare earth export controls threaten manufacturing supply chains. Oil shock from Hormuz adds energy cost pressure.
supporting_evidence: China sanctioned 50 Japanese companies including rare earth controls
Oil price surge increases energy import costs for resource-poor Japan
counter_arguments: Defense spending boost adds fiscal stimulus
estimates.investor_implication: Japan growth outlook weakening; supply chain disruption from China sanctions is near-term risk. Defensive quality stocks preferred.

estimates.reasoning: Japan CPI at 3.3% expected to moderate to 2.1% in 2026 per IMF. BOJ normalization at 0.25% rate is cautious. Oil price surge could delay disinflation but base effects support moderation.
supporting_evidence: Base effects from 2025 energy price spikes fade
counter_arguments: Oil price surge from Hormuz adds new energy cost pressure
estimates.investor_implication: Inflation moderating toward BOJ target; energy price shock may delay but not derail disinflation trend.

estimates.reasoning: Japan current account surplus at 4.82% of GDP. Strong income account from overseas investments offsets trade pressures. China sanctions could reduce bilateral trade.
supporting_evidence: Primary income account remains strong from overseas assets
Services trade improving with tourism recovery
counter_arguments: China rare earth controls threaten manufacturing exports
Higher energy import costs from oil shock
China sanctions reduce bilateral trade flows
estimates.investor_implication: Current account surplus resilient due to investment income; trade balance faces headwinds from China sanctions and energy costs.

estimates.reasoning: Japan debt at 229.6% of GDP -- highest globally -- continues rising. Defense spending increase to 2% of GDP target by 2027 adds fiscal burden. BOJ normalization raises debt servicing costs.
supporting_evidence: Defense spending rising to 2% target two years ahead of schedule
BOJ rate normalization increases debt servicing costs
Aging population increases social security expenditure
counter_arguments: BOJ still largest holder of JGBs
Low interest rates historically contained servicing costs
estimates.investor_implication: Japan debt sustainability depends on BOJ policy; rate normalization creates arithmetic risk. Watch JGB market stability.

estimates.reasoning: Japan political stability at 0.95 remains high. PM Takaichi's assertive stance on Taiwan defense raised China tensions but domestic support for defense buildup is broad.
supporting_evidence: Broad public support for defense modernization
Constitutional reform debate ongoing but manageable
counter_arguments: Defense policy shifts creating some domestic debate
estimates.investor_implication: Political stability remains Japan's strength; institutional quality supports long-term investment thesis.

estimates.reasoning: Japan military spending at 1.4% of GDP accelerating toward 2% target, achieved two years ahead of schedule. China sanctions and Taiwan contingency planning drive buildup. Largest defense expansion since WWII.
supporting_evidence: Japan hit 2% defense spending target two years early
China sanctions on Japanese military-linked entities
PM Takaichi's Taiwan defense comments triggered China response
counter_arguments: Constitutional constraints on military use remain
Public opinion mixed on rapid militarization
estimates.investor_implication: Defense sector expansion is structural; Japanese defense contractors (Mitsubishi Heavy, Kawasaki Heavy) benefit from multi-year buildup.

estimates.reasoning: Japan trade openness faces pressure from China sanctions on rare earths and dual-use goods. Supply chain restructuring away from China reduces trade efficiency. Hormuz oil disruption affects energy imports.
supporting_evidence: Rare earth export controls threaten high-tech manufacturing
Oil import disruption from Hormuz crisis
counter_arguments: US-Japan alliance provides alternative supply chains
estimates.investor_implication: Trade openness under structural pressure from China decoupling; supply chain resilience investments needed.

estimates.reasoning: Japan FDI inflows stable. Weak yen makes Japanese assets attractive for foreign buyers. China tensions may redirect some China-bound FDI to Japan as alternative Asian manufacturing hub.
supporting_evidence: Weak yen creates bargain opportunities for foreign investors
China-Japan tensions may redirect some FDI
counter_arguments: Energy costs rising from oil shock
estimates.investor_implication: Japan FDI stable with potential upside from China-alternative positioning; semiconductor and defense sectors attract capital.

estimates.reasoning: JPY at 156.9/USD. BOJ normalization supports yen but interest rate differential with Fed remains wide. Safe-haven flows during Iran crisis provide some yen support.
supporting_evidence: Yen benefits from safe-haven status during Iran crisis
counter_arguments: Wide rate differential with Fed (0.25% vs 4.5%)
Energy import costs increase yen supply
estimates.investor_implication: Yen likely range-bound 150-160; BOJ pace of normalization is key variable. Yen weakness benefits exporters.

estimates.reasoning: Japan investment risk modestly increasing from China sanctions, energy shock, and regional military tensions. Strong institutions and governance quality provide offset.
estimates.investor_implication: Japan risk modestly elevated; quality bias warranted. Defense and domestic-oriented sectors preferred over China-exposed manufacturers.

estimates.reasoning: Japan-China relations severely deteriorated. China sanctioned 50 Japanese companies, restricted rare earth exports, and imposed dual-use goods controls. This is the worst bilateral moment in decades.
supporting_evidence: Rare earth and dual-use export controls imposed
Response to PM Takaichi's Taiwan defense comments
counter_arguments: Economic interdependence creates mutual incentive to limit escalation
estimates.investor_implication: Japan-China economic decoupling accelerating; investors must assess supply chain exposure to Chinese rare earths and components.

estimates.reasoning: Germany GDP growth at 0.2% expected to recover to 0.9% in 2026. ECB rate cuts support recovery. Eurozone inflation at 1.7% provides space for further easing. However, oil shock from Hormuz and China slowdown pose downside risks to export-dependent economy.
supporting_evidence: Eurozone inflation fell to 1.7%, lowest since Apr 2021
counter_arguments: Oil shock raises energy costs for industrial economy
China slowdown affects major export market
EU-US trade deal vote postponed, tariff uncertainty persists
estimates.investor_implication: Germany recovery slow but directionally positive; cyclical industrials may underperform energy-sensitive peers.

estimates.reasoning: Germany CPI at 2.1%, forecast to moderate to 1.8%. Eurozone disinflation trend supports ECB easing. Oil shock from Hormuz could add near-term pressure.
counter_arguments: Oil price surge from Hormuz adds energy cost pressure
Wage negotiations in key sectors ongoing
estimates.investor_implication: Inflation near target; oil shock is key risk variable. ECB rate path remains accommodative.

estimates.reasoning: Germany current account surplus at 5.77% stable. Strong export base maintained but EU-US trade deal postponement and China slowdown moderate outlook.
counter_arguments: Oil shock raises energy import costs
estimates.investor_implication: Trade surplus resilient but composition shifting; energy costs eroding industrial margin.

estimates.reasoning: Germany debt at 64.4% stable near Maastricht 60% threshold. Constitutional debt brake limits fiscal expansion despite infrastructure investment needs and defense spending increase to 2.1%.
supporting_evidence: Fiscal conservatism embedded in coalition politics
estimates.investor_implication: Germany's fiscal position remains strong relative to peers; Bund safe-haven status intact.

estimates.reasoning: Germany political stability at 0.59 stable. Coalition governance continues. EU-level policy coordination on trade and defense provides institutional framework.
estimates.investor_implication: Political stability adequate for investment; watch state-level elections for populist trends.

estimates.reasoning: Germany military spending at 2.1% crossed NATO 2% target. European security concerns from Russia-Ukraine war and broader geopolitical instability drive continued buildup.
counter_arguments: Debt brake constrains rapid spending increases
estimates.investor_implication: Defense spending growth structural; Rheinmetall and European defense firms benefit from multi-year order pipeline.

estimates.reasoning: Germany trade openness at 80% of GDP remains high but faces headwinds from EU-US trade friction and China decoupling. EU-US trade deal postponement adds uncertainty.
supporting_evidence: EU single market provides trade base
SCOTUS tariff ruling reduces worst-case US tariff scenario
counter_arguments: China sanctions on Japan signal broader trade fragmentation
estimates.investor_implication: Trade openness structurally high but quality of trade shifting; supply chain resilience becoming priority.

estimates.reasoning: Germany FDI inflows stable. Strong manufacturing base and EU market access attract investment despite energy cost concerns.
counter_arguments: High energy costs post-Russia gas cutoff
estimates.investor_implication: FDI stable; greenfield investment shifting toward services; manufacturing FDI faces energy cost headwinds.

estimates.reasoning: EUR at 0.85/USD (1.17 USD/EUR). ECB rate cuts relative to Fed support some euro weakness but safe-haven flows during Iran crisis are mixed. Eurozone disinflation provides easing space.
supporting_evidence: ECB cutting rates while Fed holds
USD safe-haven demand during Iran crisis
counter_arguments: EU current account surplus supports euro
ECB rate cuts already priced in
estimates.investor_implication: EUR/USD range-bound; ECB dovishness creates modest depreciation bias. Euro exporters benefit.

estimates.reasoning: Germany investment risk stable. Strong institutions offset economic stagnation and energy vulnerability. Oil shock adds modest risk.
counter_arguments: Energy dependency risk from oil shock
estimates.investor_implication: Germany remains core European allocation; risk is underperformance rather than loss. Infrastructure and defense offer growth.

estimates.reasoning: Germany's key bilateral relationships stable. EU partnerships strong. US trade tensions manageable after SCOTUS ruling. China relationship complex but functional.
supporting_evidence: EU partnerships deepening on defense and trade
SCOTUS ruling reduces worst-case US tariff scenario
counter_arguments: China trade tensions via Japan sanctions signal broader risk
estimates.investor_implication: Relationship stability supports trade-dependent economy; EU integration deepening is positive for German exporters.

estimates.reasoning: UK GDP growth at 1.3%, forecast stable at 1.3% for 2026. Spring Forecast and OBR projections due March 3 will update outlook. Oil shock adds energy cost pressure.
supporting_evidence: Spring Forecast imminent with updated OBR projections
counter_arguments: Inflation at 3.4% limits BOE easing space
estimates.investor_implication: UK growth modest; services-driven economy less exposed to manufacturing supply chain risks but energy costs matter.

estimates.reasoning: UK CPI at 3.4% expected to moderate to 2.5% in 2026 per IMF. BOE maintaining restrictive stance. Oil shock from Hormuz adds near-term upside risk.
counter_arguments: Oil price surge adds energy cost pressure
estimates.investor_implication: UK inflation declining but slowly; BOE rate cuts likely delayed by oil shock. Gilt yields face upward pressure.

estimates.reasoning: UK current account deficit at -2.19% stable. Financial services surplus partially offsets goods deficit. Oil shock raises energy import costs.
counter_arguments: Oil shock increases energy import bill
estimates.investor_implication: Current account deficit manageable; pound stability depends on BOE rate differential.

estimates.reasoning: UK debt at 103.4% on upward trajectory. Spring Forecast expected to confirm limited fiscal headroom. Defense spending at 2.3% and NHS costs rising.
estimates.investor_implication: Gilt market watch for Spring Forecast reaction; fiscal headroom concerns may pressure yields.

estimates.reasoning: UK political stability at 0.51 stable under Labour government. Institutional strength maintained.
estimates.investor_implication: Political stability adequate; policy continuity expected.

estimates.reasoning: UK military spending at 2.3% above NATO target. Iran conflict and Russia threat sustain defense investment.
counter_arguments: Competing NHS and social spending priorities
estimates.investor_implication: UK defense spending growth sustained; BAE Systems and Rolls-Royce defense units benefit.

estimates.reasoning: UK trade openness stable post-Brexit. New trade agreements providing modest expansion.
supporting_evidence: Post-Brexit FTAs with Australia, NZ, CPTPP accession
estimates.investor_implication: Trade openness stable; services exports growing to offset goods trade friction.

estimates.reasoning: UK FDI stable. London financial center status maintained. Spring Forecast may affect investment sentiment.
estimates.investor_implication: UK FDI resilient; financial services and tech continue to attract capital.

estimates.reasoning: GBP at 0.75/USD stable. BOE rate at 4.75% supports pound. Safe-haven flows mixed during Iran crisis.
counter_arguments: Oil shock may pressure pound via energy imports
estimates.investor_implication: Sterling range-bound; BOE rate differential is primary support factor.

estimates.reasoning: UK investment risk stable. Strong institutions and rule of law offset fiscal concerns and oil shock exposure.
estimates.investor_implication: UK risk profile stable; core allocation for European exposure with financial services tilt.

estimates.reasoning: UK bilateral relations stable. US-UK special relationship strong. EU relationship normalizing post-Brexit.
counter_arguments: EU-UK friction on Northern Ireland protocol residual
estimates.investor_implication: Relationship stability supports UK's geopolitical positioning.

estimates.reasoning: Iran GDP faces severe contraction following US-Israeli strikes that killed Supreme Leader Khamenei, destroyed key military and nuclear facilities, and triggered Hormuz closure. Succession crisis, IRGC retaliation, and international isolation compound economic damage.
supporting_evidence: Military strikes destroyed infrastructure across Tehran, Isfahan, Tabriz, Qom
counter_arguments: Oil price surge benefits Iran if any exports can resume
Domestic economy partially autarkic due to prior sanctions
estimates.investor_implication: Iran economy entering severe recession; all Iranian assets effectively untradeable for international investors.

estimates.reasoning: Iran inflation at 42.4% will surge from supply disruptions, currency collapse, import restrictions, and reconstruction costs. Succession crisis removes monetary policy coordination.
supporting_evidence: Supply chain destruction from military strikes
Currency likely collapsing on parallel markets
counter_arguments: Price controls may temporarily suppress official figures
Prior sanctions had already created parallel economy
estimates.investor_implication: Hyperinflationary pressure mounting; Iranian rial likely in freefall on unofficial markets.

estimates.reasoning: Iran current account surplus at 11.39% will collapse as Hormuz closure blocks oil exports. Energy export revenue -- the primary driver -- effectively zeroed while imports for reconstruction needs surge.
supporting_evidence: Oil was primary export revenue source
counter_arguments: Existing oil smuggling routes may partially compensate
China and Russia may provide alternative trade channels
estimates.investor_implication: Current account reversal from surplus to deficit likely if Hormuz closure persists beyond weeks.

estimates.reasoning: Iran debt at 35.6% will surge from military spending, reconstruction costs, and revenue collapse from export disruption. Monetary financing likely given institutional vacuum.
supporting_evidence: Revenue collapse from oil export disruption
Military spending surge from IRGC retaliation operations
counter_arguments: Relatively low starting debt level provides some buffer
Domestic debt denomination limits external default risk
estimates.investor_implication: Fiscal position collapsing; debt dynamics unsustainable without oil revenue restoration.

estimates.reasoning: Iran political stability at -1.69 faces catastrophic deterioration following Khamenei assassination. Succession crisis, IRGC power consolidation, potential factional conflict, and 40-day mourning period create leadership vacuum.
supporting_evidence: Supreme Leader Khamenei killed in strikes
Succession crisis with no clear heir
IRGC pledging revenge and consolidating control
counter_arguments: IRGC institutional structure may provide continuity
Revolutionary ideology may unify factions temporarily
estimates.investor_implication: Maximum political instability; Iran effectively ungovernable in near-term. Regional spillover risk highest in decades.

estimates.reasoning: Iran military spending will surge as IRGC mobilizes for sustained retaliation campaign. Defense takes absolute priority over all other spending.
supporting_evidence: IRGC declared all US regional assets legitimate targets
Retaliatory strikes launched against 27 US bases
Missile strikes on Israel and West Jerusalem
counter_arguments: Military infrastructure damaged in strikes limits spending effectiveness
estimates.investor_implication: War footing economy; all resources directed to military.

estimates.reasoning: Iran trade openness collapses with Hormuz closure, international isolation, and destruction of trade infrastructure.
counter_arguments: China and Russia may maintain some trade channels
estimates.investor_implication: Trade effectively severed for international markets.

estimates.reasoning: FDI into Iran effectively zero. Active war zone, destroyed infrastructure, succession crisis, and enhanced sanctions make investment impossible.
estimates.investor_implication: Iran FDI zero; existing foreign assets at risk of destruction or confiscation.

estimates.reasoning: Iranian rial in freefall. Central bank authority disrupted, oil revenue collapsed, capital flight accelerating.
counter_arguments: Capital controls and price fixing may mask official rate
estimates.investor_implication: Rial effectively untradeable on international markets.

estimates.reasoning: Iran investment risk at maximum level. Active war, leadership assassination, infrastructure destruction, and economic collapse.
supporting_evidence: Active military conflict with US and Israel
estimates.investor_implication: Maximum risk rating; no international investment viable.

estimates.reasoning: Iran bilateral relations in crisis. Retaliation against Gulf states hosting US bases damaged regional ties. Only Russia-China axis provides support.
supporting_evidence: IRGC attacked US bases in Qatar, Kuwait, UAE, Bahrain
Gulf states caught between Iran and US
counter_arguments: Russia and China supporting Iran diplomatically at UNSC
estimates.investor_implication: Iran isolated from Gulf neighbors; only Russia-China axis intact.

estimates.reasoning: France GDP at 0.7% forecast to improve to 0.9% in 2026. ECB easing supports recovery. Eurozone inflation at 1.7% gives monetary policy room. Oil shock is key risk.
supporting_evidence: ECB rate at 3.0% with further cuts expected
counter_arguments: Oil shock from Hormuz raises energy costs
High debt at 116.5% constrains fiscal stimulus
estimates.investor_implication: France recovery modest but positive; ECB tailwind supports rate-sensitive sectors.

estimates.reasoning: France CPI at 1.1% below ECB target. Expected to rise to 1.5% in 2026. Oil shock may accelerate reflation.
supporting_evidence: Energy prices rising from Hormuz crisis
counter_arguments: ECB easing is response to low inflation
estimates.investor_implication: Inflation below target; oil shock provides reflation push that ECB may welcome.

estimates.reasoning: France current account near balance at 0.09%. Services surplus offsets goods deficit. Oil shock adds modest pressure.
estimates.investor_implication: External balance stable; tourism provides resilience.

estimates.reasoning: France debt at 116.5% on upward trajectory. Highest in Eurozone after Italy and Greece. Defense spending at 2.1% and social spending constrain consolidation.
counter_arguments: ECB rate cuts reduce debt servicing costs
estimates.investor_implication: Fiscal sustainability concerns persist; OAT-Bund spread watch indicator.

estimates.reasoning: France political stability at 0.34 reflects fragmented National Assembly. Government functioning but major reforms difficult. Iran crisis position (UNSC emergency session co-sponsor) aligns with traditional French diplomatic activism.
estimates.investor_implication: Political stability adequate but not strong; reform capacity limited.

estimates.reasoning: France military spending at 2.1% above NATO target. Nuclear deterrent and Middle East regional engagement sustain defense investment.
estimates.investor_implication: Defense spending growth structural; Dassault, Thales, Naval Group benefit.

estimates.reasoning: France trade openness stable. EU single market provides base. EU-US trade deal postponement adds uncertainty.
France FDI stable. Second largest EU economy attracts investment despite political uncertainty.
estimates.investor_implication: FDI resilient; tech and luxury sectors attract capital.

estimates.reasoning: EUR/USD at 0.85 (1.17 USD/EUR). Same dynamics as Germany -- ECB easing vs Fed hold creates modest depreciation bias.
estimates.investor_implication: EUR modestly weaker; benefits French exporters.

estimates.reasoning: France investment risk stable. Strong institutions offset fiscal concerns. Oil shock and Iran crisis add modest risk premium.
